title: Count Matching Subsequences

content.problemStatement:
Given two strings S1S1S1 and S2S2S2 of length MMM and NNN, find the number of different subsequences of S1S1S1 that match exactly with S2S2S2. Return the answer modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

First line contains TTT, the number of test cases
Each of the next TTT lines contains two space-separated strings s1s1s1 and s2s2s2


Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer representing the count of subsequences modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7
constraints: All characters are lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space-separated strings s1 and s2 consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of distinct subsequences of s1 that are exactly equal to s2, modulo 1 000 000 007.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Only one way to pick characters a‑c‑e from "abcde".
Test case 2: The single character 'a' can be taken from any of the three positions.
The whole string matches exactly, so there is exactly one subsequence.
Both strings consist of a single identical character.

hidden.label: stress case
hidden.input:
3
abc abc
abcde ace
aaa a

hidden.label: stress case 3
hidden.input:
5
a a
ab ab
abc abc
aaa a
aab abb

solution.approach: Let dp[i][j] be the number of ways the prefix of length i of s1 can form the prefix of length j of s2. The transition is: if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1] then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j]; otherwise dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]. The base case is dp[i][0] = 1 for all i (empty s2 can be formed in exactly one way) and dp[0][j] = 0 for j>0. The answer is dp[|s1|][|s2|] modulo 1e9+7. To reduce memory we keep only two rows of size |s2|+1.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i: test case index (0 <= i <= 20)
    # RINT(a,b): random integer in [a,b]
    # POWER(a,b): a**b
    if i == 0:
        print(1)
        print('a a')
    elif i == 1:
        print(1)
        print('a b')
    # Add generation logic for other indices as needed
